title: What does the sleepless version of me deserve to hear?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for writing the message you would send back to the years of difficult sleep.
excerpt: A journal prompt set from Day 16 of the SLEEP program. Five prompts for identifying what you needed to hear during the hard sleep years — and what you can offer that version of yourself now.
sourceNotes: Adapted from Day 16 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use alongside the Day 16 tapping practice, or any time you want to process what the difficult sleep years cost and what they deserved.

content.text: A journal prompt set from Day 16 of the SLEEP program. The point is not to relitigate the difficult years but to offer them something — a perspective, a word of acknowledgement, a small piece of what they were owed.
New to journalling as a practice? Read
What was the hardest part of the bad sleep years that didn't get named at the time?
Write the specific thing — not a general description of tiredness but the particular cost that didn't get acknowledged.
What did you say to yourself about your body during those years?
Write the truth of it. The critical thoughts, the frustration, the blame. They don't need defending or justifying — just putting down.
What would the version of you in the middle of those years have needed to hear?
Write the message. Not reassurance or a promise about the future — something real and present that she could have held.
What do I still carry from those years that I could put down?
Write what comes. The vigilance, the shame, the body-blame — whatever still runs even now.
What would I say to myself tonight as an act of kindness for those years?
Write the sentence. Small and specific is better than large and general.
Adapted from Day 16 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Program (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
